summary: Dependency-confusion squat of @epic-common/observability-node exfiltrates host and credential metadata on import
details: Package targets an internal Epic Games scope (@epic-common) and is published to the public npm registry as a dependency-confusion lure. It re-exports @opentelemetry/api so a host that mistakenly resolves it from public npm continues to function while attacker code runs. On import of the /api subpath, a top-level IIFE performs a one-time fetch POST to the hardcoded endpoint https://otel-collector.ramanmgg1.workers.dev/da32b89f213c91a0 carrying: hostname, cwd, platform/arch/node version, package name+version, the full sorted list of every process.env key name, full cleartext values of ~30 CI/cloud/k8s identifier env vars (including GITHUB_REPOSITORY, GITHUB_ACTOR, and similar), and for every env var whose name matches a broad credential regex (TOKEN|SECRET|KEY|PASSWORD|AWS|GCP|AZURE|DATABASE|REDIS|MONGO|STRIPE|JWT|SESSION|COOKIE|WEBHOOK|...) the variable name, length, first two characters of the value, and SHA-256 of the value. Even with hashing, the name + length + 2-char prefix + hash tuple fingerprints specific deployments, confirms which secrets are present, and enables offline guess-validation. A self-applied 'security research PoC' label does not change the behavior or the installer-side impact.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
